turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has our opinion this morning in case 141 on the original docket, Texas v. New Mexico and Colorado.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: The water of the Rio Grande subject to many and competing demands so much so that Will Rogers once called it the only river I ever saw in need of irrigation. To resolve ongoing disputes over the river's scarce resources back in the 1930s Colorado, New Mexico and Texas with Congress' approval signed the Rio Grande Compact. The compact requires Colorado to deliver a specified amount of water annually to New Mexico at the state line and directs New Mexico in turn to deliver a specified amount of water to the Elephant Butte Reservoir in New Mexico. In turn the compact anticipates that the federal government will use some of the reservoir's resources to supply water both to Mexico under a treaty and to certain downstream Texas water districts under a series of contracts between those districts and the federal government. Recently Texas brought this action in our Court complaining that New Mexico is violating the compact by allowing New Mexico to siphon off water intended for downstream users like Texas' water districts and Mexico. The United States intervened and filed a complaint with parallel allegations. The Special Master filed a report suggesting that Texas should be allowed to proceed with its lawsuit but also suggesting that the United States' complaint should be dismissed in part because the compact does not confer on the federal government the power to enforce its terms. This Court agreed to hear two exceptions to the report concerning the scope of the claims United States may assert. Today we hold that the United States may pursue the compact claims it has pleaded in this original action. This Court using its unique authorities to mold original actions and disputes between the states has sometimes permitted the federal government to participate in compact suits to defend distinctively federal interests that a normal litigant might not be permitted to pursue in traditional litigation. While this permission should not be confused with license several considerations taken collectively lead us to the conclusion that United States may pursue the particular claims that is pleaded in this case. First the compact is inextricably intertwined with the contracts on which the government is legally bound to deliver water to Texas from the Elephant Butte Reservoir. Because of them you might think of the federal as sort of agent of the compact ensuring that the promises to Texas are fulfilled or you might think the federal government's contracts has incorporated into the compact. Either way it's clear that the federal government's duties under its contracts are essential to the compact's operation. Second, New Mexico has conceded in pleadings and oral argument that the United States plays an integral role in the compact's operation. Third, a breach of the compact could reduce the water available in the reservoir in ways that could jeopardize the federal government's ability to satisfy its treaty obligations to Mexico. Fourth, the United States has asserted its compact claims in an existing action brought by Texas, seeking substantially the same relief and without that state's objection. So this case does not present the question whether the United States could initiate litigation to force a state to perform obligations of the contract will expand the scope of an existing controversy between the states. For these reasons considered collectively the United States' exception to the Special Master's report is sustained and all other exceptions are overruled. The case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The decision of the court is unanimous.
